summarization.a: Figure 12 illustrates the concentration gradient of N₂ mole fraction along the reagent-purge boundary in reactor R4. The heatmap shows the small micrometer-scale variations in N₂ concentration, highlighting the limited spatial resolution available for capturing the temporal progression of surface coverage. These small length scales make it challenging to collect surface coverage data at high angular velocities and short time scales, which contributes to the discontinuous curves observed in Figure 11. The figure emphasizes the spatial difficulty in simulating adsorption dynamics near the boundary region.

d.question: How does understanding the N₂ concentration gradient in Figure 12 assist in optimizing surface coverage simulations?
d.answer: The N₂ concentration gradient highlights the spatial limitations for collecting accurate surface coverage data along the boundary region. By understanding these small-scale gradients, researchers can adjust simulation parameters, such as time step and nodal resolution, to better capture adsorption dynamics. This insight helps explain the discontinuous curves seen in Figure 11 and guides the design of more precise ASALD simulations, ensuring accurate prediction of surface coverage and uniform film deposition under different operating conditions.

b.question: How does the N₂ concentration change along the reagent-purge boundary in reactor R4?
b.answer: The N₂ mole fraction gradually increases from 0.450 to 1.000 along the boundary, illustrating a smooth concentration gradient across micrometer-scale positions.

a.question: the small micrometer-scale length along the reagent-purge boundary affect the ability to collect surface coverage data?

c.question: What factors contribute to the discontinuous coverage curves observed in Figure 11 according to the description?
c.answer: Small spatial length scales (micrometers) along the reagent-purge boundary, Limited nodal data collection at these small scales, High angular velocities reducing exposure time, Constraints in temporal resolution (milliseconds) for capturing adsorption dynamics
